metadata.summary: GitLab CI/CD is an integrated DevSecOps platform using .gitlab-ci.yml to define DAG-based pipelines with stages, jobs, rules, and environments. Use the needs keyword to enable parallelism and replace sequential stages. Always use protected, masked CI/CD variables for secrets.

content.title: GitLab CI/CD
content.summary: GitLab CI/CD is an integrated DevSecOps platform using .gitlab-ci.yml to define pipelines with stages, jobs, rules, and environments. Use DAG with the needs keyword to replace sequential stages and achieve parallelism. Never store secrets in .gitlab-ci.yml — use protected, masked CI/CD variables. GitLab 18 (2025) adds immutable artifact tags and structured pipeline inputs.

why.p: GitLab CI/CD provides a single platform for source control, pipelines, container registry, environments, DORA metrics, and security scanning — eliminating the integration overhead of separate tools. The needs keyword unlocks DAG-based pipelines that run 2–5x faster than sequential stages by parallelizing independent jobs.

list.item: Projects hosted on GitLab (cloud or self-hosted)
Teams needing integrated DevSecOps: SAST, DAST, dependency scanning, secret detection
Multi-environment deployments with review apps per merge request
Monorepo projects with change-based job filtering (rules: changes)
Repositories hosted on GitHub — use GitHub Actions instead
Simple single-developer projects — overhead of .gitlab-ci.yml is not justified; use a Makefile
Projects requiring Jenkins-level plugin customization not available in GitLab

rule.statement: Use the `needs` keyword to define job dependencies explicitly instead of relying on sequential stages.
rule.rationale: Sequential stages force all jobs in one stage to complete before any job in the next stage starts. The `needs` keyword unlocks DAG execution: independent jobs run in parallel while dependent jobs wait only for their direct dependencies.

rule.statement: Set `interruptible: true` on all build and test jobs so newer pipeline runs can cancel stale ones.
rule.rationale: Without `interruptible: true`, pushing multiple commits in quick succession causes all pipelines to run fully, wasting runner capacity on intermediate commits that are already superseded.

rule.statement: Define `workflow: rules` at the top of `.gitlab-ci.yml` to control when a pipeline is created; target: default branch, merge requests, and tags only.
rule.rationale: Without workflow rules, GitLab creates a pipeline for every branch push including feature branches without merge requests, causing unnecessary CI runs.

example.title: Standard workflow rules
example.note: Standard workflow rules covering main branch, MR pipelines, and release tags.

rule.statement: Use `extends` with hidden job templates (`.template-name`) to share configuration across jobs; use YAML anchors only for single-file reuse.
rule.rationale: `extends` merges deeply across the entire pipeline definition and works with GitLab's `include` for cross-file sharing. YAML anchors only work within the same file.

rule.statement: Key caches on dependency lock files (`package-lock.json`, `Gemfile.lock`, `requirements.txt`) to invalidate automatically when dependencies change.
rule.rationale: Keying on the lock file hash means cache misses occur exactly when they should (dependency update) and hits occur on all other runs without manual cache management.

rule.statement: Use `policy: pull` for jobs that only read the cache and `policy: pull-push` for the job that builds it; define a fallback key for initial cache population.
rule.rationale: Without pull-only policy, every parallel job tries to update the same cache key simultaneously, causing write contention on distributed cache backends (S3/GCS).

rule.statement: Mark all secret CI/CD variables as Protected (available only on protected branches) and Masked (hidden in job logs).
rule.rationale: Unprotected variables are accessible in any branch pipeline, including attacker-controlled branches. Unmasked variables appear in job logs visible to all project members.

rule.statement: Never echo or print secret variables in pipeline scripts; use `--silent` flags with curl and avoid `set -x` in scripts that handle secrets.
rule.rationale: Shell debug mode (`set -x`) prints every command with expanded variable values to the job log, exposing secrets to anyone with log access.

rule.statement: Use file-type CI/CD variables for certificates, kubeconfig, and private keys — they are written to a temp file and the path is the variable value.
rule.rationale: Multiline secrets in string variables are fragile across shells. File-type variables handle encoding correctly and are automatically cleaned up after the job.

rule.statement: Include GitLab security templates via `include:` rather than installing scanners manually — they receive automatic updates from GitLab.
rule.rationale: Manually maintained scanner installations drift in version and configuration over time. GitLab's managed templates stay current with the scanner vendor's latest recommendations.

example.title: GitLab security scanning templates
example.note: Include all four GitLab security scanning templates.
example.code: include: - template: Security/SAST.gitlab-ci.yml - template: Security/Dependency-Scanning.gitlab-ci.yml - template: Security/Container-Scanning.gitlab-ci.yml - template: Security/Secret-Detection.gitlab-ci.yml

antipattern.description: Hardcoding credentials or API keys in `.gitlab-ci.yml`.
antipattern.reason: `.gitlab-ci.yml` is committed to Git. Even if the repository is private, any team member or compromised account can read the file and extract credentials.

rule.statement: Deploy review apps for every merge request using `CI_MERGE_REQUEST_IID` as the environment name suffix; set `auto_stop_in: 1 week` to prevent orphaned namespaces.
rule.rationale: Review apps let reviewers test changes in a live environment before merge. Without auto_stop_in, each merged or abandoned MR leaves a running environment that consumes cluster resources indefinitely.

rule.statement: Always pair a review-deploy job with a review-stop job (same environment name, `action: stop`, `when: manual`) to enable manual early cleanup.
rule.rationale: Auto-stop waits for the timeout; manual stop lets developers clean up immediately after review is complete, reclaiming resources and reducing cloud costs.

rule.statement: Require manual approval (`when: manual`) for all production deployments; automate staging deployments on default branch.
rule.rationale: Automated production deployments without a human gate increase blast radius for any CI/CD misconfiguration or untested code path that slips past automated checks.

rule.statement: Set `environment.url` on all deployment jobs so GitLab tracks the live URL and shows it in MR and environment views.
rule.rationale: Without the URL, team members cannot navigate to the deployed environment directly from GitLab, reducing visibility into what is actually deployed.

example.title: Review app deploy and stop with auto-stop
example.note: Review app deploy and stop jobs with auto-stop after one week.
example.code:
review-deploy: stage: deploy environment: name: review/$CI_MERGE_REQUEST_IID url: https://$CI_MERGE_REQUEST_IID.review.example.com on_stop: review-stop auto_stop_in: 1 week script: - kubectl create namespace review-$CI_MERGE_REQUEST_IID --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f - - envsubst < k8s/review/deployment.yaml | kubectl apply -f - - kubectl rollout status deployment/app -n review-$CI_MERGE_REQUEST_IID --timeout=5m rules: - if: $CI_MERGE_REQUEST_IID
review-stop: stage: deploy variables: GIT_STRATEGY: none environment: name: review/$CI_MERGE_REQUEST_IID action: stop script: - kubectl delete namespace review-$CI_MERGE_REQUEST_IID --ignore-not-found when: manual rules: - if: $CI_MERGE_REQUEST_IID

example.title: Node.js DAG Pipeline
example.note: Node.js pipeline with DAG: lint and test run in parallel after install, build runs after both pass.
example.code:
default: image: node:20-alpine
cache: key: files: [package-lock.json] paths: [node_modules/, .npm/]
workflow: rules: - if: $CI_COMMIT_BRANCH == $CI_DEFAULT_BRANCH - if: $CI_MERGE_REQUEST_IID
stages: [install, test, build, deploy]
install: stage: install script: npm ci --cache .npm --prefer-offline artifacts: paths: [node_modules/] expire_in: 1 hour
lint: stage: test needs: [install] script: npm run lint interruptible: true
test: stage: test needs: [install] script: npm test -- --coverage interruptible: true artifacts: reports: coverage_report: coverage_format: cobertura path: coverage/cobertura-coverage.xml
build: stage: build needs: [test, lint] script: npm run build artifacts: paths: [dist/] expire_in: 1 week

example.title: Docker Build with Trivy Scan
example.note: Docker build with BuildKit cache, Trivy vulnerability scan blocking on CRITICAL findings.
example.code:
variables: DOCKER_TLS_CERTDIR: "/certs" IMAGE_TAG: $CI_REGISTRY_IMAGE:$CI_COMMIT_SHA IMAGE_LATEST: $CI_REGISTRY_IMAGE:latest
docker-build: stage: build image: docker:24 services: [docker:24-dind] variables: DOCKER_BUILDKIT: 1 before_script: - docker login -u $CI_REGISTRY_USER -p $CI_REGISTRY_PASSWORD $CI_REGISTRY script: - docker build --cache-from $IMAGE_LATEST --build-arg BUILDKIT_INLINE_CACHE=1 --tag $IMAGE_TAG --tag $IMAGE_LATEST . - docker push $IMAGE_TAG - docker push $IMAGE_LATEST
trivy-scan: stage: scan image: name: aquasec/trivy:latest entrypoint: [""] needs: [docker-build] script: - trivy image --exit-code 0 --severity HIGH,CRITICAL $IMAGE_TAG - trivy image --exit-code 1 --severity CRITICAL $IMAGE_TAG

example.title: Monorepo Change Detection
example.note: Only run API service jobs when files in `services/api/` or `shared/` change.
example.code:
.service-template: rules: - changes: - $SERVICE_PATH/**/* - shared/**/*
api-test: extends: .service-template stage: test variables: SERVICE_PATH: services/api script: cd services/api && npm test
api-build: extends: .service-template stage: build variables: SERVICE_PATH: services/api needs: [api-test] script: - cd services/api - docker build -t $CI_REGISTRY_IMAGE/api:$CI_COMMIT_SHA . - docker push $CI_REGISTRY_IMAGE/api:$CI_COMMIT_SHA

section.name: agent-integration-notes
section.p: When to use: Repo lives on GitLab (SaaS or self-hosted) and the team already uses GitLab issues / MRs / Container Registry — pipeline lives next to the code. Need a single platform that bundles SCM + CI + registry + DAST/SAST + Pages + Kubernetes Agent without gluing 5 SaaS tools together. Pipelines need fan-out / parent-child / multi-project triggering — GitLab's `trigger:` and `include:` are first-class, simpler than reusable workflows in GHA for cross-repo use. Compliance environments where every stage must produce an artifact / SBOM / scan report attached to a pipeline that auditors can pull from one place. Self-hosted runners on K8s with the GitLab Runner Helm chart — autoscaling executor pods per job is painless.

When NOT to use: Codebase on GitHub or Bitbucket — using GitLab CI as an external runner via mirror/webhook is technically possible but creates a dual-source-of-truth headache. Tiny throwaway projects — `.gitlab-ci.yml` syntax has more surface area (rules, workflow, includes, extends) than a one-job GHA workflow. Teams that need OSS-friendly free tier matching GHA — GitLab.com free CI minutes are tighter; for public repos GHA is genuinely free.

Common limitations: `rules:` vs `only/except` confusion: agents copy old `only/except` snippets from blogs, which silently get overridden by `workflow:rules`. Force `rules:` only. Stages without `needs:` serialize the whole pipeline. Default templates in old projects do this and waste 60%+ of wall time. `dependencies:` vs `needs:artifacts:` — both pass artifacts but `needs` activates DAG. Mixing them produces non-obvious "where did my file go" bugs. Cache vs artifacts: agents reach for `artifacts:` when they want `cache:` (or vice versa). Artifacts are uploaded to GitLab on every job; caches are runner-local by default — cache key + policy matters. `extends:` plus YAML anchors (`&`/`*`) plus `include:` with overrides → final job spec is unreadable. Agents lose track of what actually runs. Always run `gitlab-ci-lint` and inspect the merged config. Protected branches / protected variables: secrets only flow on protected refs. Agents test on a feature branch, secret is empty, job fails with cryptic "permission denied". Self-hosted Docker executor with `dind` is slow and racy. Prefer Kubernetes executor with BuildKit, or use rootless `kaniko`/`buildah`. GitLab runner concurrency setting is per-runner-process, not per-project — easy to over-saturate a self-hosted box.

Agentic workflow: Have one agent draft the pipeline structure (stages, jobs, DAG via `needs:`, env-aware `rules:`) as a Markdown table BEFORE writing YAML — this catches "this job has no real trigger" at design time. A second agent generates `.gitlab-ci.yml` using `extends:` for shared steps and a small `include:` library. Always pipe the result through `glab ci lint` (or `POST /ci/lint`) inside the agent loop; YAML that lints clean still fails on `rules:` semantics, so trigger a sandboxed run on a draft MR before merging. A reviewer agent inspects merged config (`glab ci config`) and the timing/parallelism actually achieved on first run.

template.purpose: Standard Node.js pipeline with install/lint/test/build/deploy stages and DAG
Docker build with BuildKit cache, Trivy scan, and registry push
Review app deploy/stop lifecycle with auto_stop_in and namespace cleanup
GitLab security template includes: SAST, dependency, container, secret detection
